event: Early hominins inhabited the site of Dmanisi, leaving some of the oldest human fossils found outside Africa.
Neolithic farming communities of the Shulaveri-Shomu culture cultivated crops and domesticated animals in the South Caucasus.
Some of the world's earliest known winemaking took place in the region, evidenced by residue in clay vessels.
The Kura-Araxes (Early Transcaucasian) culture flourished across Georgia during the Early Bronze Age.
The Trialeti and later Colchian cultures developed advanced bronze metallurgy in western and central Georgia.
The Kingdom of Colchis emerged on the eastern Black Sea coast, later famed in Greek myth as the land of the Golden Fleece.
Greek colonists from Miletus founded coastal trading settlements such as Phasis and Dioscurias in Colchis.
The Kingdom of Iberia (Kartli) was founded in eastern Georgia, traditionally under its first king Pharnavaz I.
Roman general Pompey campaigned in the Caucasus, bringing Iberia and Colchis into Rome's sphere of influence.
King Pharasmanes I of Iberia expanded the kingdom's power and intervened in Armenian and Parthian affairs.
Iberia adopted Christianity as its state religion through the preaching of Saint Nino, making Georgia one of the earliest Christian nations.
Iberia fell increasingly under the control of the Sasanian Persian Empire, which appointed governors over the kingdom.
King Vakhtang I Gorgasali led a major revolt against Sasanian Persia and is credited with founding Tbilisi.
The Sasanians abolished the Iberian monarchy, replacing it with rule by local princes (erismtavari).
Arab armies conquered Tbilisi, beginning centuries of Islamic influence and the eventual Emirate of Tbilisi.
Ashot I founded the Bagrationi dynasty's rule in Tao-Klarjeti, establishing a line that would unify Georgia.
Bagrat III united eastern and western Georgian lands into a single Kingdom of Georgia.
King David IV the Builder decisively defeated the Seljuk Turks at the Battle of Didgori.
David IV recaptured Tbilisi from the Muslim emirate and made it the capital of the unified kingdom.
Queen Tamar ascended the throne and presided over the Georgian Golden Age of cultural and military greatness.
Around the end of Tamar's reign, the poet Shota Rustaveli composed the national epic 'The Knight in the Panther's Skin'.
The Khwarazmian Empire under Jalal al-Din sacked Tbilisi, devastating the Georgian kingdom.
The Mongols invaded and subjugated Georgia, reducing it to a tributary state.
Timur (Tamerlane) began a series of devastating invasions of Georgia that lasted into the early 15th century.
Georgia fragmented into the rival kingdoms of Kartli, Kakheti, and Imereti along with several principalities.
The Peace of Amasya divided Georgian lands between Ottoman and Safavid Persian spheres of influence.
Shah Abbas I of Persia launched brutal invasions of Kakheti, deporting and massacring much of its population.
Erekle II united the kingdoms of Kartli and Kakheti into a single eastern Georgian state.
Erekle II signed the Treaty of Georgievsk, placing Kartli-Kakheti under Russian protection.
Persian ruler Agha Mohammad Khan invaded and sacked Tbilisi, devastating the city.
The Russian Empire annexed the Kingdom of Kartli-Kakheti, abolishing the Georgian monarchy.
Russia annexed the Kingdom of Imereti, extending imperial control over western Georgia.
Serfdom was abolished in Georgia, reshaping the country's rural society under Russian rule.
Georgia declared independence as the Democratic Republic of Georgia following the collapse of the Russian Empire.
The Red Army invaded and conquered Georgia, incorporating it into Soviet rule.
Georgia became part of the Soviet Union within the Transcaucasian Socialist Federative Soviet Republic.
Georgia became a full constituent republic of the USSR as the Georgian Soviet Socialist Republic.
Soviet troops violently suppressed protests in Tbilisi sparked by Khrushchev's denunciation of Stalin.
Soviet forces killed peaceful pro-independence demonstrators in Tbilisi on April 9, galvanizing the independence movement.
Georgia declared independence from the Soviet Union, with Zviad Gamsakhurdia elected as first president.
Georgia joined the United Nations as a sovereign state amid civil war and the rise of Eduard Shevardnadze.
Georgia lost control of Abkhazia after a separatist war that displaced hundreds of thousands of people.
The peaceful Rose Revolution ousted Shevardnadze and brought reformist Mikheil Saakashvili to power.
A brief war with Russia over South Ossetia led Moscow to recognize Abkhazia and South Ossetia as independent.
The Georgian Dream coalition won parliamentary elections, marking Georgia's first peaceful transfer of power via the ballot.
Georgia signed an Association Agreement with the European Union, deepening its Western integration.
Georgia was granted European Union candidate status, though political tensions over the country's direction intensified.
